exercises.title: Calculate optimal pool size
exercises.instructions:
DB server: 4 cores, SSD. Service has 5 instances. Each instance uses HikariCP.
1. Calculate optimal pool size per instance
2. Total DB connections
3. Is this within PostgreSQL default max_connections=200?
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Pool size per instance: (4 cores × 2) + 1 (SSD spindle) = 9 → round to 10

2. Total DB connections: 5 instances × 10 pool = 50 connections

3. Within limit: 50 of 200 max_connections = 25% utilization → comfortable.
   If you needed 20 instances: 20 × 10 = 200 → AT THE LIMIT.
   Use PgBouncer for connection multiplexing when approaching max_connections.

test_cases.expected:
1. Pool size per instance: (4 cores × 2) + 1 (SSD spindle) = 9 → round to 10

2. Total DB connections: 5 instances × 10 pool = 50 connections

3. Within limit: 50 of 200 max_connections = 25% utilization → comfortable.
   If you needed 20 instances: 20 × 10 = 200 → AT THE LIMIT.
   Use PgBouncer for connection multiplexing when approaching max_connections.

hints: Formula: (cores × 2) + spindles
SSD: spindle_count ≈ 1 (no physical disk spindles)
Total = instances × pool_size_per_instance

exercises.title: Complete HikariCP application.yml
exercises.starter_code:
spring.datasource.hikari:
  maximum-pool-size: ___
  minimum-idle: ___
  connection-timeout: ___   # ms: fail fast, not hang
  max-lifetime: ___          # ms: 30 min, less than DB wait_timeout
exercises.expected_solution:
spring.datasource.hikari:
  maximum-pool-size: 10
  minimum-idle: 2
  connection-timeout: 3000   # ms: fail fast, not hang
  max-lifetime: 1800000      # ms: 30 min, less than DB wait_timeout
hints: connectionTimeout: how long app waits for a free connection from pool
max-lifetime: max age of a connection — must be < DB's own connection timeout

exercises.title: Monitor HikariCP metrics in Spring Boot
exercises.instructions:
Show how to access HikariCP metrics via:
1. /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.active
2. Prometheus PromQL for pool utilization
3. Grafana alert rule for pool exhaustion
exercises.expected_solution:
# HikariCP metrics auto-registered by Spring Boot Actuator + Micrometer:
# GET /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.active
# GET /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.idle
# GET /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.pending
# GET /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.acquire  (Timer: time to acquire a connection)

# Prometheus text format at /actuator/prometheus:
# hikaricp_connections_active{pool='PaymentServicePool'} 8.0
# hikaricp_connections_idle{pool='PaymentServicePool'} 12.0
# hikaricp_connections_pending{pool='PaymentServicePool'} 0.0
# hikaricp_connections_max{pool='PaymentServicePool'} 20.0

# PromQL — Pool utilization (% of connections in use):
# hikaricp_connections_active / hikaricp_connections_max * 100
# Alert: > 90% for 2 minutes → pool nearly exhausted

# PromQL — Connection wait time (p99):
# histogram_quantile(0.99,
#   sum(rate(hikaricp_connections_acquire_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, pool)
# )
# Alert: p99 acquire time > 1s → threads waiting too long for connections

# prometheus/rules.yml:
# - alert: HikariPoolExhaustion
#   expr: hikaricp_connections_pending > 5
#   for: 30s
#   labels: { severity: warning }
#   annotations:
#     summary: 'Connection pool has >5 pending requests'
#     runbook_url: 'https://wiki.razorpay.com/runbooks/pool-exhaustion'

public class HikariMetricsDemo { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("hikaricp.connections.*: auto-registered by Spring Boot"); } }
hints: HikariCP metrics: auto-registered when spring-boot-starter-actuator + HikariCP on classpath
connections.pending: key metric — rising = pool exhaustion approaching
pool-name in hikari config: appears as 'pool' label in Prometheus metrics

exercises.title: Detect connection leak with leakDetectionThreshold
exercises.instructions:
Write HikariCP config to detect connection leaks:
1. leakDetectionThreshold: 2000ms
2. Show: what the log message looks like when a leak is detected
3. Common causes of connection leaks in JPA
4. Fix pattern
exercises.expected_solution:
# application.yml:
spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      leak-detection-threshold: 2000  # ms: warn if connection held > 2s
      # Note: set to 0 in production (slight performance overhead)
      # Use during development/staging to find leaks

# LEAK DETECTION LOG OUTPUT (when triggered):
# WARN  c.z.h.p.ProxyLeakTask - Connection leak detection triggered for
#   com.razorpay.PaymentRepository#findAll() on thread request-3.
#   The stack trace below may help identify the connection usage location.
#   java.lang.Exception: Apparent connection leak detected
#     at com.razorpay.service.PaymentService.processPayment(PaymentService.java:42)
# This means: connection was checked out at line 42 and not returned for 2+ seconds

# COMMON CONNECTION LEAK CAUSES:
# 1. @Transactional missing on a method that calls JPA
#    JPA opens a new connection for each operation (never pooled together)
#    Fix: add @Transactional to the service method
#
# 2. Exception in @Transactional method without proper catch
#    Spring rolls back, but HikariCP still gets the connection returned
#    Usually not a leak — Spring handles this
#
# 3. JDBC connection from DataSource used directly, not closed:
#    Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection();
#    // ... code without try-with-resources ...
#    Fix: try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection()) { ... }
#
# 4. Spring Data JPA Stream<> not closed:
#    Stream<Payment> stream = repo.findAll()
#    // stream.forEach() but stream not closed → connection held!
#    Fix: try (Stream<Payment> stream = repo.findAll()) { ... }

public class LeakDetection { public static void main(String[] a) {
    System.out.println("leakDetectionThreshold: 2s for dev/staging, 0 for prod"); } }
hints: leakDetectionThreshold: 2000ms in dev to find leaks early
Stream<>: JPA streaming queries hold connection open — always use try-with-resources
@Transactional: ensures connection returned to pool after method completes

exercises.title: Configure multiple DataSources with different pool sizes
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay has 2 databases: payments (primary, write-heavy) and analytics (read-heavy).
1. Configure 2 DataSources with different HikariCP settings
2. payments: small pool (write-heavy, short queries)
3. analytics: larger pool (read-heavy, longer queries)
4. @Configuration class
hints: @Primary: Spring uses this DataSource when multiple exist and no specific one requested
@ConfigurationProperties: binds YAML properties to the DataSourceProperties bean
Different pool sizes: analytics can handle more parallel queries (read-only, scalable)

exercises.title: Pool exhaustion simulation and circuit breaker
exercises.instructions:
Write a test that simulates pool exhaustion:
1. Inject DataSource, get multiple connections without releasing
2. Observe: connectionTimeout triggers after 3 seconds
3. Show: circuit breaker pattern to handle pool exhaustion gracefully
4. @SpringBootTest: verify connectionTimeout fires
hints: connection.close(): returns connection to pool (not actually closed)
connectionTimeout in test: may need to set low value in test application.yml
SQLTransientConnectionException: HikariCP's exception when pool exhausted

exercises.title: HikariCP metrics @SpringBootTest
exercises.instructions:
Write @SpringBootTest that:
1. Injects MeterRegistry
2. Looks up hikaricp.connections.active and hikaricp.connections.idle
3. Verifies metrics are registered (Spring Boot auto-wires this)
4. Exercises a JPA query and checks active connections change
hints: hikaricp.connections.max: always registered (reflects maximumPoolSize)
active + idle = total connections (may be < max if minimum-idle not yet reached)
acquire timer: only populated after at least one connection acquisition

exercises.title: PgBouncer connection pooling overview
exercises.instructions:
Document when and how to use PgBouncer:
1. Why: 10 service instances × 20 pool = 200 connections (DB max!)
2. PgBouncer: multiplexes many app connections to fewer DB connections
3. PgBouncer modes: session vs transaction vs statement
4. Spring Boot configuration for PgBouncer-backed connection
5. Application.yml change required
hints: Transaction mode: compatible with most Spring Data JPA usage
PgBouncer port: 6432 (default), connects to PostgreSQL on 5432
provider_disables_autocommit: critical for transaction mode compatibility

exercises.title: Tune HikariCP for batch processing vs real-time
exercises.instructions:
Razorpay has two workloads:
1. Real-time payment service: latency-critical (< 100ms), short queries
2. Reconciliation batch: throughput-critical, long transactions

For each: write the HikariCP configuration explaining the differences.
exercises.expected_solution:
# REAL-TIME PAYMENT SERVICE (latency-critical):
spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 10
      minimum-idle: 5         # keep 5 warm connections (reduce latency on startup)
      connection-timeout: 3000  # fail FAST — don't queue threads for > 3s
      idle-timeout: 60000       # close idle connections quickly (1 min)
      max-lifetime: 1800000     # 30 min: rotate connections regularly
      keepalive-time: 30000     # 30s keepalive: prevent firewall from killing idle
      pool-name: PaymentPool
      # Result: low latency (warm connections), fast failure on DB pressure

---
# RECONCILIATION BATCH (throughput-critical):
spring:
  config.activate.on-profile: reconciliation
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20       # larger pool: more parallel batch queries
      minimum-idle: 10            # keep many warm (batch needs burst capacity)
      connection-timeout: 30000   # 30s OK: batch can wait for connection (not user-facing)
      idle-timeout: 600000        # 10 min: keep connections during batch pause periods
      max-lifetime: 1800000       # same: 30 min
      transaction-isolation: READ_COMMITTED  # batch: allow dirty reads for performance
      pool-name: ReconciliationPool
      # Result: high throughput (large pool), tolerates wait (not user-facing)

# KEY DIFFERENCES:
# Real-time: small pool (low latency per query), fast fail, short idle
# Batch: large pool (high parallelism), can wait, long idle (batch runs in phases)
hints: Real-time: connectionTimeout=3000 (fail fast to preserve circuit breaker pattern)
Batch: connectionTimeout=30000 OK (no human waiting, throughput matters)
minimum-idle: higher for batch (pre-warm more connections for burst)

exercises.title: Pool exhaustion incident diagnosis
exercises.instructions:
At 7pm, Grafana shows:
```
hikaricp_connections_active = 10/10 (all connections in use)
hikaricp_connections_pending = 25 (25 threads waiting)
hikaricp_connections_acquire_seconds max = 28.3s
```

1. What is happening?
2. What caused this?
3. Immediate mitigation
4. Permanent fix
exercises.expected_solution:
1. WHAT IS HAPPENING:
   Pool is completely exhausted: all 10 connections in use.
   25 threads waiting > 28 seconds for a free connection.
   connectionTimeout must be set to >= 30s (old misconfiguration).
   All 25 waiting threads: will throw ConnectionTimeoutException after ~30s.
   During those 30s: 25 Tomcat threads blocked → service partially unavailable.

2. WHAT CAUSED THIS:
   7pm: traffic spike (typical payment peak in India).
   Higher concurrency → all 10 DB connections occupied simultaneously.
   connectionTimeout=30s: threads queue instead of failing fast.
   Root causes (pick one):
     a) Pool too small for the traffic volume
     b) Slow DB queries holding connections too long
     c) Missing index → query takes 500ms instead of 5ms → same connection needed 100× longer

3. IMMEDIATE MITIGATION:
   a) Increase pool size: kubectl set env deployment/payment-service HIKARI_MAX_POOL_SIZE=20
   b) Restart pods to apply (rolling restart: no downtime)
   c) Check for slow queries: SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active'

4. PERMANENT FIX:
   a) Set connectionTimeout=3000 (3 seconds) — fail fast, enable circuit breaker
   b) Increase pool size to formula result for current load
   c) Find and index the slow query (if query duration is the root cause)
   d) Add HikariPool exhaustion alert: connections_pending > 5 for 30s

test_cases.expected:
1. WHAT IS HAPPENING:
   Pool is completely exhausted: all 10 connections in use.
   25 threads waiting > 28 seconds for a free connection.
   connectionTimeout must be set to >= 30s (old misconfiguration).
   All 25 waiting threads: will throw ConnectionTimeoutException after ~30s.
   During those 30s: 25 Tomcat threads blocked → service partially unavailable.

2. WHAT CAUSED THIS:
   7pm: traffic spike (typical payment peak in India).
   Higher concurrency → all 10 DB connections occupied simultaneously.
   connectionTimeout=30s: threads queue instead of failing fast.
   Root causes (pick one):
     a) Pool too small for the traffic volume
     b) Slow DB queries holding connections too long
     c) Missing index → query takes 500ms instead of 5ms → same connection needed 100× longer

3. IMMEDIATE MITIGATION:
   a) Increase pool size: kubectl set env deployment/payment-service HIKARI_MAX_POOL_SIZE=20
   b) Restart pods to apply (rolling restart: no downtime)
   c) Check for slow queries: SELECT * FROM pg_stat_activity WHERE state='active'

4. PERMANENT FIX:
   a) Set connectionTimeout=3000 (3 seconds) — fail fast, enable circuit breaker
   b) Increase pool size to formula result for current load
   c) Find and index the slow query (if query duration is the root cause)
   d) Add HikariPool exhaustion alert: connections_pending > 5 for 30s

hints: connections_pending=25 + acquire=28s: threads waiting almost connectionTimeout
connectionTimeout=30s is the misconfiguration: allows long queue
3 approaches: increase pool, fix slow query, fail fast + circuit breaker

exercises.title: Complete HikariCP health indicator
exercises.instructions:
Write HikariPoolHealthIndicator:
1. Implements HealthIndicator
2. Reports: active, idle, pending, max connections
3. Returns DOWN if pending > 5
4. @SpringBootTest: verify health indicator is UP with details
5. Shows in /actuator/health as 'hikariPool'
hints: @Component("hikariPool"): appears as 'hikariPool' in /actuator/health
pending > 5 → DOWN: signals pool exhaustion to readiness probe
withDetail: visible in health endpoint with show-details: always

exercises.title: Complete M22 T3 pool monitoring synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete HikariCP monitoring:
1. PoolMonitoringService: gauge for utilization %, counter for timeout events
2. HikariPoolHealthIndicator (DOWN if pending > 5)
3. Pool size configuration (4-core DB, SSD, 3 service instances)
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): health UP, gauge registered

All 2 tests pass.
hints: utilization: active/max — 0 if no active connections yet
hikaricp.connections.pending: 0 in quiet test = health UP
Gauge value 0: connections.active=0 in test context is expected

exercises.title: Zerodha trading: complete pool tuning
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha has 2 DBs: orders (write-heavy) + market-data (read-heavy).
1. Two DataSource configs with different pool sizes
2. Pool monitor gauges for both pools
3. Alert rule for each pool
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): both pools registered
5. All tests pass
hints: Two gauges: one per pool, tagged by pool name
.tag("pool", poolName): filter metrics by pool name
util() returns 0 in test — both pools may have 0 active connections

exercises.title: Validate connection validity with keepalive
exercises.instructions:
Configure and validate HikariCP keepalive to prevent stale connections:
1. keepalive-time: 60s (ping DB every minute)
2. connection-test-query: SELECT 1
3. validation-timeout: 2000ms
4. Show: what happens without keepalive (firewall kills idle connections)
5. @SpringBootTest: DataSource is reachable
hints: keepalive-time: prevents firewall from killing 'idle' connections
validation-timeout: max time for SELECT 1 validation
max-lifetime < firewall-idle-timeout: rotate connections before firewall kills them

exercises.title: Complete M22 T3 HikariCP synthesis — pool + monitoring + health
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Razorpay HikariCP observability:
1. Correct pool configuration (4-core DB, SSD)
2. Pool monitoring gauge (utilization)
3. Health indicator (DOWN if pending > 5)
4. Connection validity test (SELECT 1)
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): gauge, health, connection valid

All 3 pass.
hints: utilization may be 0 in test (no active connections) — assert 0 ≤ u ≤ 1.0
pending=0 in test → health is UP (correct normal state)
SELECT 1: lightest possible DB validation query
